Very strong winds developed over portions of the Front Range mountains and foothills.  Peak wind gusts included:  98 mph, 2 miles south-southeast of Gold Hill, 86 mph atop Berthoud Pass, 83 mph atop Peak 8 at Breckenridge Ski Area with 75 mph, 3 miles east of Gold Hill.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall to locations in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Storm totals included:  9 inches in Louisville, 8.5 inches at Lafayette and 2 miles south of Rocky Flats, 8 inches, 2 miles south of Boulder and 3 miles north-northeast of Eldorado Springs; 7.5 inches in Erie, 7 inches, 3 miles west-northwest of Arvada and at the National Weather Service in Boulder; 6 inches at Copeland Lake, Evergreen, 3 miles northwest of Idledale and Intercanyon.
Behind a cold front, high winds on the backside of a weak upper level disturbance reached the ground in Kit Carson County.  The peak gust was 63 MPH at both Flagler and Seibert.

Beginning March 6th, and lasting into mid May, severe drought was reported along and south of Highway 40 in Cheyenne County.  Total precipitation from around the county in March ranged from 0.02 to 0.26.
Heavy snow accumulations on a weak snowpack resulted in a fatal avalanche near Red Mountain Pass in the western San Juan Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A backcountry skier was killed in an avalanche he triggered within an area locally know as Sam's Trees in the Chattanooga area between Red Mountain Pass and the town of Silverton.  The avalanche location was close to Highway 550.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40-50 MPH produced near zero visibility in blowing dust on I-70 between Bethune and Stratton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility reported in patchy blowing dust across I-70 between Bethune and Stratton.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system that moved over the Pacific Northwest and across the Northern Rockies brought a period of high winds to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills.  Peak wind gusts included:  101 mph atop Berthoud Pass; 97 mph near Loveland Pass and 2 miles south-southeast of Gold Hill; 80 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab southwest of Boulder and 2 miles southwest of Rocky Flats; and 75 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland.  The high winds also toppled a large tree at the mouth of Boulder Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate to heavy snow moved in from the west beginning in the afternoon and lasting through the evening.  With wind gusts of 30 to over 50 MPH, visibility under the band of snow fell to zero.  The highest snowfall amounts occurred over Yuma County where 10 inches of snow was reported near Vernon.  The snowfall amounts decreased to the south with the western part of Cheyenne County reporting only two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 3 to almost 13 inches from west to east.  A passenger bus and a semi were involved in an accident on I-70 at mile marker 418.  Of the 53 passengers, only a few injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system tracked from the Four Corners region eastward across Colorado.  Consequently,  period of moderate to heavy snow developed in and near the foothills of Jefferson and Douglas counties.  In the foothills, storm totals included:  17 inches, 4 miles south-southeast of Pinecliffe; 15 inches, 3 miles west of Conifer; 14 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 13.5 inches, 13 miles northwest of Golden; 12 inches in Genesee;  11.5 inches at Breckenridge; 11 inches at Roach and Deadman Hill SNOTELs; 10.5 inches, 3 miles northwest of Idledale; with 9 inches, 3 miles west-southwest of Golden, Lake Irene SNOTEL and Silverthorne.  Across Denver and its western and southern suburbs and the Palmer Divide, storm totals included:  8.5 inches near Rocky Flats and 5 miles west-northwest of Arvada; 8 inches in Wheat Ridge; 7.5 inches in Federal Heights and 4 miles northwest of Elizabeth; 7 inches at Manila Village and near Greenwood Village; 6.5 inches in Westminster; 6 inches at Castle Pines, 3 miles south-southwest of Denver and Northglenn;  and 5.7 inches at Denver International Airport.  Elsewhere,  storm totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 8 to 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system produced significant to heavy snow across portions of western Colorado mostly within a 24-hour period from Saturday, January 20th to Sunday, January 21st.  The snowfall persisted into Sunday night mainly over some northwest facing mountain slopes. Some lower elevation precipitation began as rain on Saturday and then changed over to snow with the passage of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 7 to 17 inches across the area, including 12 to 17 inches on Colorado Highway 550 from just below Ouray to Red Mountain Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Badger Hole Wildfire ignited on April 17th and spread quickly across southeast Colorado (Baca County) and western Kansas fueled by strong winds, low RH, and dry fuels. The fire burned around 50,000 acres total, including 33,609 acres in Colorado (the rest in Kansas). This makes it the 10th largest wildfire in Colorado recorded history. The fire was contained on April 19th, and destroyed multiple homes, outbuildings, livestock and infrastructure. The cause is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds blew across portions of the Grand Valley in western Colorado on Saturday evening, May 26th as a result of outflow winds from high-based isolated showers or virga. The strong winds resulted in significant property damage in some areas within the eastern end of the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to an estimated 50 to 60 mph across the eastern end of Grand Valley from approximately 30 to 32 Road and from D to E Roads. There were numerous reports from social media of damage to roofs and of trees being blown down. Tree branches ranging from 4 to 8 inches in diameter snapped. One of the snapped tree limbs fell on a car near 30 Road and D 1/2 Road and caused significant damage. Additionally, a large portion of fencing was blown down at the Shadowbrook subdivision near Patterson and 31 Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging tornadoes, straight-line winds, large hail and heavy rain across northeast Colorado.  A total of 4 tornadoes touched down and impacted the communities of Brush, Hillrose, and Snyder.  An EF-2 tornado struck the east side of Brush and caused significant damage to the Brush Airport and turned semi-trucks on their side on U.S. 34.  Highway 34, was closed near the Morgan-Washington county line due to debris and downed power lines.  In Hillrose and Snyder moderate damage to structures included:  roofs, windows, siding, broken trees, power lines and piles down.  In Snyder, the town hall was also damaged and some roadways were inundated with over 3 feet of standing water.  In Snyder, a tornado touched down in the Pinneno feedlot, which injured and killed livestock.   Heavy agricultural damage to crops was reported and pivots were destroyed. ||In Morgan County, over 200 poles down were knocked down and caused extensive power outages across the area.   Those affected by the tornados were expected to be without power for up to 2 days.  Approximately 20 people suffered mainly minor injuries.   The hail ranged in size from quarter to baseball size, and produced peak wind gusts to around 75 mph.  Multiple road closures were reported around Morgan County due to flooded roadways and downed power lines.   As the storm continued east, near-zero visibility rain fell in Akron, which was accompanied by strong winds.  In the southern portion of Washington County, the storm produced hail up to baseball size south of Anton. There was also widely spread damage to crops, vehicles, roofs, siding, and windows.  In Greeley, reports of damage included broken windshields and destroyed gardens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down and uprooted many trees, with debris striking vehicles.

Storm totals included:  16.5 inches, 4 miles southeast of Mount Zirkel; 16 inches at Copper Mountain; 14 inches at A-Basin Ski Area; 12 inches at Breckenridge Summit; 10 inches near Mount Audobon; 9 inches, 7 miles south-southeast of Cameron Pass; 8 miles northwest of Glendevy, Keystone Ski Area and Loveland Pass.   In addition to the heavy snow, very strong winds were also observed with gusts ranging from 50 to 65 mph above timberline.

High winds, combined with blowing snow, created blizzard conditions across East Central Colorado during April 13th-14th. The blizzard closed many of the area highways and I-70 from Limon to the Kansas border.  The high winds and blowing snow also caused power outages in Yuma County.  The highest wind gust reported was 74 MPH at the Burlington airport.  Snowfall amounts ranged from around an inch to nine inches, with the highest snowfall reported near Burlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread power outages reported throughout the county.  A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was reported near Idalia at 3:53 PM MT.  Emergency manager, and the public from different parts of the county, reported blizzard conditions.  Three to nine inches of snowfall was reported across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported across the county.  I-70 was shut down from the Kansas border to Limon.  A peak wind gust of 74 MPH was reported at the Burlington airport.  Snowfall across the county ranged from three to seven inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced winds, gusting to 80 mph at times over portions of southern Colorado.  Wind gusts between 58 mph and 65 mph were recorded near Walsenburg, Springfield, Hoehne, Sargents, Cheraw, Peterson Air Force Base, Lamar and Fountain.  Wind gusts between 66 mph and 70 mph were measured near Weston and the Air Force Academy.  Finally, a wind gust that clocked 80 mph was realized near the Pikes Peak summit.

A strong cold front brought high winds, blowing dust, and a wildfire to Eastern Colorado. The high winds led to some wind damage across the area. In addition, the winds caused widespread blowing dust. Finally, at least one fire was noted in Eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 78 mph measured 3 WNW of Burlington at 3:10 pm MST. Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with some wind damage reported. A stock tank was blown across the road 8 miles north of Burlington on HWY 385.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed across Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties in the morning.  The high winds caused blowing dirt to reduce the visibility to zero at times.  Highway 40/287 was closed due to a semi blown over and the blowing dust.  The peak gust for the day was 71 MPH measured in Firstview.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest wind gust was measured in Firstview.  Highway 287-40 was closed due to a semi that was blown over.  The high winds also created zero visibility in the blowing dust.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire southwest of Norwood in a remote area on Bureau of Land Management land near Disappointment Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horse Park Fire began during a lightning event 15 miles southwest of Norwood and grew to a total of 1,221 acres by the time it was contained in June. A resource truck got stuck and firefighters were forced to abandon it. The truck was later consumed by the fire. This fire persisted into June.

Very large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter, pummeled portions of the Front Range Urban Corridor and extended across the northeast plains of Colorado.   Reports of collapsed roofs due to hail were reported, with major hail damage across northern portions of the Denver metro area.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Association estimated the property damage from the storm totaled $276.4 million, making it the 8th costliest hailstorm to strike the state to date.  Frontage roads along Interstate 76 northeast of Denver were also flooded and washed out.  In Lincoln County, large hail in the Arriba and Genoa areas damaged vehicles along Interstate 70. A total of four short-lived tornadoes touched down in the open country of Lincoln and Weld counties.  Thunderstorms in Lincoln County also produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 mph.

The storms that moved across northern Washington County caused severe agricultural damage. Wheat fields were significantly damaged and the young corn was stripped of all its leaves. Although no roads were completely washed out, there were multiple places where water ran across the roads and washed away the gravel and damaged the shoulders. One house at State Highway 61 and county road 57 had at least one broken window from the hail.   Most of the water and damage from these storms was between state highways 61 and 63, and between county roads 60 and 55.||In Weld County, several communities received hail damage and flooding due to severe thunderstorms:  Frederick, Firestone, Hudson, Keenesburg and Prospect Valley. Lightning struck an injection well northeast of Hudson, sparking a fire.  First responders worked this incident for several hours.  Weld County had several road closures due to flash flooding including Hudson, Keenesburg, Roggen and Prospect Valley.  Some of the road closures included:  county road 46 from between county roads 45 to 47, county road 63 from county roads 16 to 16.5.  Trained spotters measured storm total rainfall from 2.93 inches to 5.53 inches.

Some of the road closures included:  county road 46 from between county roads 45 to 47, county road 63 from county roads 16 to 16.5.  Trained spotters measured storm total rainfall from 2.93 inches to 5.53 inches.

The storms that moved across northern Washington County caused severe agricultural damage. Wheat fields were significantly damaged and the young corn was stripped of all its leaves. Although no roads were completely washed out, there were multiple places where water ran across the roads and washed away the gravel and damaged the shoulders. One house at State Highway 61 and county road 57 had at least one broken window from the hail. Two groups of thunderstorms moved east across East Central Colorado during the afternoon.  The first group was larger and to the north of the second.  It produced hail up to quarter size north of Bethune.  This group intensified as it crossed into Kansas.  The second smaller group moved across Cheyenne County.  Hail up to nickel size was reported with these storms, along with wind gusts up to 70 MPH.

Two groups of thunderstorms moved east across East Central Colorado during the afternoon.  The first group was larger and to the north of the second.  It produced hail up to quarter size north of Bethune.  This group intensified as it crossed into Kansas.  The second smaller group moved across Cheyenne County.  Hail up to nickel size was reported with these storms, along with wind gusts up to 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea to nickel in size and was accompanied by estimated wind gusts of 70 MPH and torrential rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front pushed across western Colorado and produced moderate snowfall across some mountain areas. Gusty winds accompanied the front which led to some areas of reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area.  Wind gusts ranged from 20 to 40 MPH with areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued: A rare nocturnal hail event impacted El Paso County during the early morning hours(MDT) of Wednesday, June 13th, 2018, producing hail up to 3 inches in diameter.  Media reports indicate that this event caused $169 million in damages to vehicles, trees, roofs, siding, windows, skylights and other parts of homes.  Some of the larger reported hailstones with this event during the early morning of the 13th included hail the size of hen eggs at Widefield, hail the size of tennis balls at Security and hail the size of tea cups in Fountain and Peterson Air Force Base.

Deep moisture associated with the remnants of what was once Hurricane Bud spread north into western Colorado. This resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock and other debris flowed just south of the Iron Horse Inn along and onto Highway 550 as a result of heavy rainfall. CoCoRaHS observers reported 0.70 inches of rain fell with storms near that area.

Two groups of thunderstorms moved east across East Central Colorado during the afternoon.  The first group was larger and to the north of the second.  It produced hail up to quarter size north of Bethune.  This group intensified as it crossed into Kansas.  The second smaller group moved across Cheyenne County.  Hail up to nickel size was reported with these storms, along with wind gusts up to 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were gusting to atleast 60 MPH.  Pea size hail also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east into East Central Colorado during the latter half of the afternoon.  The largest hail reported occurred at Abarr.  The highest wind gust reported was 67 MPH at the Burlington airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quite a bit of roof and siding damage along with broken windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare nocturnal hail event impacted El Paso County during the early morning hours(MDT) of Wednesday, June 13th, 2018, producing hail up to 3 inches in diameter. Media reports indicate that this event caused $169 million in damages to vehicles, trees, roofs, siding, windows, skylights and other parts of homes.  Some of the larger reported hailstones with this event during the early morning of the 13th included hail the size of hen eggs at Widefield, hail the size of tennis balls at Security and hail the size of tea cups in Fountain and Peterson Air Force Base.

Deep moisture associated with the remnants of what was once Hurricane Bud spread north into western Colorado. This resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused a mud to flow across the westbound lane of Interstate 70 at the Avon Exit 167.  That portion of I-70 was closed for about 50 minutes until the debris was removed. Radar estimated storm total rainfall was up to 0.50 of an inch in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of June. As a result, drought conditions in southwest Colorado either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the central portion of the zone and expanded slightly northwest during the month.  Exceptional (D4) drought conditions in some of the southern portion of the zone also expanded slightly northward towards the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of June. As a result, drought conditions in southwest Colorado either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the end of the month and expanded to encompass the northwest corner of the zone towards the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of June. As a result, drought conditions in southwest Colorado either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the Delta County portion of the Grand Mesa through the month.

A rare nocturnal hail event impacted El Paso County during the early morning hours(MDT) of Wednesday, June 13th, 2018, producing hail up to 3 inches in diameter. Media reports indicate that this event caused $169 million in damages to vehicles, trees, roofs, siding, windows, skylights and other parts of homes.  Some of the larger reported hailstones with this event during the early morning of the 13th included hail the size of hen eggs at Widefield, hail the size of tennis balls at Security and hail the size of tea cups in Fountain and Peterson Air Force Base.

A wildfire began from unknown causes 13 miles north of Durango. The fire burned on the west side of Colorado Highway 550 initially on private land and then onto the San Juan National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 416 fire began from unknown causes 13 miles north of Durango. The fire burned on the west side of Colorado Highway 550 on some private land and within the San Juan National Forest and persisted throughout the month of July. It grew to a total of 54,129 acres before it was contained at the end of July. Road closures occurred along Highway 550 at times due to fire operations as well as in portions of the San Juan National Forest. Operations of the Durango and Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad were halted until mid July. Numerous homes were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke settled into the southwest San Juan Mountains and the Animas River Basin during the overnight and early morning hours from a combination of the 416 and Burro wildfires in the San Juan Mountains. Although widespread dense smoke diminished by the mid-morning hours, localized areas of dense smoke persisted throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke occurred across much of the southwest San Juan Mountains in La Plata County. The NWS Incident Meteorologist assigned to the 416 Wildfire confirmed visibilities dropped below a 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 117 Wildfire ignited on April 17th just off of I-25 exit 117 (hence the name), south of Fountain, CO in southern El Paso County. The car eventually spread into northern Pueblo County as well. A car dragging a muffler down the interstate caused sparks that ignited the fire, as witnessed by multiple people. Strong winds, low RH, and dry fuels allowed the fire to spread rapidly. The fire was 100% contained on April 19th after burning 42,795 acres, making it the 8th largest wildfire in Colorado recorded history. The fire destroyed 18 homes, multiple outbuildings, and caused the evacuation of around 1,000 people. The fire is estimated to have caused $1.3 million worth of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke repeatedly settled into the southwest San Juan Mountains and the Animas River Basin during the overnight and early morning hours from a combination of the 416 and Burro wildfires in the San Juan Mountains. Widespread dense smoke usually diminished by the mid-morning hours, although localized areas of dense smoke persisted throughout each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke occurred each night and through the early morning hours across much of the Animas River Basin. The NWS Incident Meteorologist assigned to the 416 Wildfire confirmed visibilities dropped below a 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from unknown causes 23 miles north of Craig and spread across a mix of private and Bureau of Land Management lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Divide Fire began from unknown causes 23 miles north of Craig and grew to a total of 19,955 acres. This fire persisted into July.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire approximately 6.5 miles northwest of Norwood which spread across a mix of private and Bureau of Land Management lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Mailbox Fire was ignited by lightning approximately 6.5 miles northwest of Norwood and grew to over 470 acres before it was contained.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Spring Creek wildfire began on June 27 around 3:57 PM 5 miles northeast of Ft Garland, CO in Costilla County, and would eventually spread into Huerfano County as well. The fire was ignited and spread through dry fuels in dry and windy weather. The fire was human caused, and the suspect was arrested. By the end of July, the fire had burned 108,045 acres, the third largest wildfire in Colorado recorded history. The fire was 91% contained, but was no longer growing and had been removed from InciWeb. The Spring Creek Wildfire destroyed at least 225 structures, 145 of which were homes, and cost an estimated $32M. The fire forced the evacuation of multiple communities and caused multiple highway closures. No causalities were reported. At it's peak, over 1,000 people were working on the fire.

CZ_NAME: TELLER COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE ABOVE 7500 FT / PIKES PEAK BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chateau fire was started by humans on June 29, 7 miles northwest of Cripple Creek, CO in Teller County. The fire was 100% contained on July 9, and was determined to be human caused. 1423 acres burned, at least 8 homes and numerous outbuildings were destroyed, and hundreds of people were evacuated. Dry fuels, low RH, and gusty winds helped the fire spread.

CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Carson Midway fire, started accidentally by an Army aviation training exercise on the Fort Carson Military base, ignited on March 16. The fire, fueled by strong winds, low humidity, and dry vegetation, quickly spread off base, burning 400 acres on Fort Carson and 2,900 acres outside of Fort Carson. The fire destroyed two homes, and forced hundreds to evacuate temporarily. The fire was contained on March 18, except for continued burning of some tires, which were extinguished on March 22.
The Bocco Fire, which was suspected to be human caused, was ignited 3 miles northwest of Wolcott.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bocco Fire began 3 miles northwest of Wolcott near the shooting range. It grew to a total of 415 acres before it was contained. An evacuation was ordered for the Alkali Creek neighborhood but no structures were damaged or lost.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire southwest of Norwood in a remote area on Bureau of Land Management land near Disappointment Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horse Park Fire began at the end of May during a lightning event 15 miles southwest of Norwood and continued to expand to a total of 1,221 acres before it was contained.  A resource truck got stuck and firefighters were forced to abandon it. The truck was later consumed by the fire.

CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 117 Wildfire ignited on April 17th just off of I-25 exit 117 (hence the name), south of Fountain, CO in southern El Paso County. The car eventually spread into northern Pueblo County as well. A car dragging a muffler down the interstate caused sparks that ignited the fire, as witnessed by multiple people. Strong winds, low RH, and dry fuels allowed the fire to spread rapidly. The fire was 100% contained on April 19th after burning 42,795 acres, making it the 8th largest wildfire in Colorado recorded history. The fire destroyed 18 homes, multiple outbuildings, and caused the evacuation of around 1,000 people. The fire is estimated to have caused $1.3 million worth of damage.

A group of thunderstorms developed directly behind a cold front and moved across East Central Colorado, expanding in coverage it moved east.  Wind gusts up to 70 MPH were reported in two different places in Yuma County as the line of storms moved through.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball southwest of Idalia.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SAN JUAN MOUNTAINS ABOVE 10000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of storm systems pummeled the Wolf Creek Pass area with an additional 20 inches of snow.  In addition, six of of snow was also measured near Hillside in Custer county with this event.

A quick moving storm system dropped six inches of snow near the communities of Maysville, Texas Creek and Colorado Springs.  Seven inches of snow graced Rye and Wetmore.  Eight inches of snow was measured near Beulah, while 14 inches of snow was recorded at Monarch Pass.
A storm system generated between six and seven inches of snow near the communities of Divide, Rye, Palmer Lake, Beulah, Florissant, Wetmore and Westcliffe.

Very large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter, pummeled portions of the Front Range Urban Corridor and extended across the northeast plains of Colorado.   Reports of collapsed roofs due to hail were reported, with major hail damage across northern portions of the Denver metro area.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Association estimated the property damage from the storm totaled $276.4 million, making it the 8th costliest hailstorm to strike the state to date.  Frontage roads along Interstate 76 northeast of Denver were also flooded and washed out.  In Lincoln County, large hail in the Arriba and Genoa areas damaged vehicles along Interstate 70. A total of four short-lived tornadoes touched down in the open country of Lincoln and Weld counties.  Thunderstorms in Lincoln County also produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 mph.

Continued: A rare nocturnal hail event impacted El Paso County during the early morning hours(MDT) of Wednesday, June 13th, 2018, producing hail up to 3 inches in diameter.  Media reports indicate that this event caused $169 million in damages to vehicles, trees, roofs, siding, windows, skylights and other parts of homes.  Some of the larger reported hailstones with this event during the early morning of the 13th included hail the size of hen eggs at Widefield, hail the size of tennis balls at Security and hail the size of tea cups in Fountain and Peterson Air Force Base.

A moist and strong southwesterly flow associated with an area of low pressure moving into the Four Corners region resulted in significant to heavy snowfall for most Colorado mountains and some of the higher elevation valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches occurred across the area.  Highway 139 over Douglas Pass was slick and snowpacked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and strong southwesterly flow associated with an area of low pressure moving into the Four Corners region resulted in significant to heavy snowfall for most Colorado mountains and some of the higher elevation valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches.  Locally higher amounts included 15 inches at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and strong southwesterly flow associated with an area of low pressure moving into the Four Corners region resulted in significant to heavy snowfall for most Colorado mountains and some of the higher elevation valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches across the area.  Locally higher amounts included 12 inches at Snowmass Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought widespread snowfall and localized blizzard conditions to much of western Colorado. Snow initially began in southwesterly flow across the high terrain of southwestern Colorado late Sunday night, February 18th, before the front moved through on Monday, February 19th. Travel was severely impacted over the high mountain passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches across the area. Blowing snow occurred with the passage of a snow band Sunday evening which led to reduced visibilities with wind gusts of 35 to 45 MPH.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though February was rather active with snowfall events, precipitation remained well below normal for the month over portions of western Colorado, and this contributed to the already drier than normal conditions prior to February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions that had been in place all winter worsened in mid February when the situation deteriorated to the extreme (D3) drought category across the area. The extreme drought conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon a supercell thunderstorm, with a history of producing large hail, moved southeast across parts of Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  Hail up to baseball size was reported south of Vona.  The highest wind gust reported was 75 MPH north of Cheyenne Wells.  The supercell continued producing significant hail in West Central Kansas.

In the late afternoon a supercell thunderstorm, with a history of producing large hail, moved southeast across parts of Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  Hail up to baseball size was reported south of Vona.  The highest wind gust reported was 75 MPH north of Cheyenne Wells.  The supercell continued producing significant hail in West Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind speeds were estimated to be 75 MPH.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of 2018 saw the arrival of a strong cutoff low pressure system into the southern Great Basin. While the northern portion of the trough produced light snowfall over the northern mountains, the cutoff low produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the San Juan Mountains and the southern valleys. Moderate snow also fell in the central mountains and valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 22 inches of snow fell across the area. Both the Vallecito and Weminche Creek SNOTEL sites estimated 30 inches of storm total snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity moved south across East Central Colorado in the early afternoon behind an outflow boundary originating from the incoming thunderstorm activity.  As the afternoon progressed the thunderstorm activity increased in coverage and intensity as the storms progressed southward.  The strongest storm was the one that became tied to the southward moving outflow boundary.  The largest hail size reported was teacup on I-70 near Flagler.  The strongest thunderstorm wind gust reported was 67 MPH near Kit Carson from this same storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail report was in I-70 near Flagler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The progression and eventual merging of two weather disturbances across western Colorado combined with a strong polar and sub-tropical jet to produce significant to heavy snowfall across most Colorado mountains and some higher valley locations. Snowfall began over the higher terrain on the evening of Wednesday, February 14th before increasing in coverage and continuing through the night of Thursday, February 15th. The heaviest snowfall fell Thursday morning and early Thursday afternoon. The support of the two upper level jets overhead enhanced snowfall rates and allowed gusty winds to mix down to the surface which resulted in periods of reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the area.  Locally higher amounts up to 8 inches were measured in the Steamboat Springs area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A particularly dangerous thunderstorm moved south across Eastern Colorado during the evening near a stationary front.  As the thunderstorm moved through Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties it produced a swath of damage from the combined effects of baseball size hail and thunderstorm wind gusts.  The highest wind gust reported was 83 MPH in the northwest part of Kit Carson County.  Aside from the structural damage, many animals in the path of this storm were killed, including nine antelope south of Seibert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A campfire got out of control and started a wildfire on an island in the Colorado River near Fruita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Skipper Island Wildfire began when a campfire got out of control on Skipper Island, located in the Horsethief Canyon State Wildlife Area. The fire quickly grew to approximately 230 acres due to strong winds. Some power poles and power lines were damaged which caused over 2,000 customers to lose power. Portions of Interstate 70 near Fruita were shut down due to dense smoke and also due to the fact that the fire jumped to the north side of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture lingered across southwest Colorado and resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving over the area produced 1.55 inches of rain in an hour and a half. Minor street and building flooding occurred with the first floor of a low lying apartment receiving some water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure spun southeast of the Four Corners region, subtropical moisture moved northward along the western periphery of the high. This increase in moisture produced some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in La Plata County. Flash flooding and significant debris flow occurred over the 416 fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms led to flash flooding at the KOA Campground 3.75 miles north-northeast of Hermosa. Radar estimates half an inch to an inch of rain fell with thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system moved into the Four Corners area and brought significant snowfall to most mountains in western Colorado. Strong southerly flow at the onset of the storm favored the San Juan Mountains.  Then the flow became more easterly and eventually northeasterly as the storm exited to eastern Colorado which allowed the significant wraparound moisture to favor the northern and central mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

These strong winds downed a power line in Archuleta County which left about 780 customers without power across La Plata and Archuleta Counties. This also led to a fire that started in the 3000 block of County Road 500. Other downed lines and equipment outages were also reported on County Roads 302 and 304 and Colorado Highway 550 in La Plata County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought widespread snowfall and localized blizzard conditions to much of western Colorado. Snow initially began in southwesterly flow across the high terrain of southwestern Colorado late Sunday night, February 18th, before the front moved through on Monday, February 19th. Travel was severely impacted over the high mountain passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches with locally higher amounts of up to a foot at the Bison Lake SNOTEL site. Peak wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph resulted in widespread blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure spun southeast of the Four Corners region, subtropical moisture moved northward along the western periphery of the high. This increase in moisture produced some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in La Plata County. Flash flooding and significant debris flow occurred over the 416 fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of mud flowed across County Road 250 as a result of heavy rain with thunderstorms. 1.48 inches of rain fell in about an hour. Debris also flowed across County Road 203. As a result of all the debris, these roads were closed for almost 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture trapped underneath a ridge of high pressure produced some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in Eagle County which led to some flash flooding over the Lake Christine fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An IRAWS station measured 0.24 inches of rain and small hail between 11:03 and 11:18 PM MST. The Incident Meteorologist on the Lake Christine fire reported that flowing water up to 6 inches deep ran across a U.S. Forest Service road and a small amount of debris flowed down drainages as a result of heavy rain.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and strong radiational cooling under clearing skies allowed low temperatures to plummet to below freezing across some of the lower western Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over half of the forecast zone had minimum temperatures down to 20 to 25 degrees F. A few locations dropped into the lower teens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought widespread snowfall and localized blizzard conditions to much of western Colorado. Snow initially began in southwesterly flow across the high terrain of southwestern Colorado late Sunday night, February 18th, before the front moved through on Monday, February 19th. Travel was severely impacted over the high mountain passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions with whiteout conditions, snowfall and blowing snow occurred across the area with widespread wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph. Measured snowfall totals only ranged from 2 to 6 inches as the snow was highly wind blown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought widespread snowfall and localized blizzard conditions to much of western Colorado. Snow initially began in southwesterly flow across the high terrain of southwestern Colorado late Sunday night, February 18th, before the front moved through on Monday, February 19th. Travel was severely impacted over the high mountain passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 5 to 11 inches across the area. Localized blizzard conditions occurred.  Peak wind gusts on mountain tops ranged from 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought widespread snowfall and localized blizzard conditions to much of western Colorado. Snow initially began in southwesterly flow across the high terrain of southwestern Colorado late Sunday night, February 18th, before the front moved through on Monday, February 19th. Travel was severely impacted over the high mountain passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches across the area with up 18 inches measured at the Weminuche Creek SNOTEL site. Localized blizzard conditions with peak wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph at highway level resulted in blowing snow and severe impacts to travel. A peak wind gust of 115 mph was measured on the top of Eagle Mountain. Colorado Highway 550 between mile markers 49 and 92 were closed due to hazardous conditions. This stretch of closed high included Red Mountain, Molas, and Coal Bank Passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of 2018 saw the arrival of a strong cutoff low pressure system into the southern Great Basin. While the northern portion of the trough produced light snowfall over the northern mountains, the cutoff low produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the San Juan Mountains and the southern valleys. Moderate snow also fell in the central mountains and valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 12 inches of snow fell at the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site with 5.4 inches reported 8 miles west-southwest of Ridgway.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of weather disturbances associated with a strong atmospheric river moved through western Colorado. This resulted in significant to heavy late-season snowfall over the northern and central Colorado mountains. The mild nature of this system kept snow levels high with the bulk of accumulations above 9,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the area. Periods of heavy snowfall rates resulted in slick and snowpacked roads which impacted travel along Interstate 70. Vail Pass was closed for over 4 hours due to multiple vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation remained below normal for many areas, even with some late season winter storms that moved through the region during the month of April. The most notable monthly deficit occurred in southwestern Colorado where precipitation was almost an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) drought conditions in mid April. This situation persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The progression and eventual merging of two weather disturbances across western Colorado combined with a strong polar and sub-tropical jet to produce significant to heavy snowfall across most Colorado mountains and some higher valley locations. Snowfall began over the higher terrain on the evening of Wednesday, February 14th before increasing in coverage and continuing through the night of Thursday, February 15th. The heaviest snowfall fell Thursday morning and early Thursday afternoon. The support of the two upper level jets overhead enhanced snowfall rates and allowed gusty winds to mix down to the surface which resulted in periods of reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although some weather systems produced significant precipitation across the area during the month of March, these systems typically favored the higher terrain. As a result, precipitation remained below normal for many areas, especially in the southwest Colorado valleys which finished the month about an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the far northwestern portion of the zone and expanded to encompass the far western areas towards the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system moved into the Four Corners area and brought significant snowfall to most mountains in western Colorado. Strong southerly flow at the onset of the storm favored the San Juan Mountains.  Then the flow became more easterly and eventually northeasterly as the storm exited to eastern Colorado which allowed the significant wraparound moisture to favor the northern and central mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches.  Locally higher amounts included 17 inches at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions that had been in place all winter worsened in mid February when the situation deteriorated to the extreme (D3) drought category across the area. The extreme drought conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity moved south across East Central Colorado in the early afternoon behind an outflow boundary originating from the incoming thunderstorm activity.  As the afternoon progressed the thunderstorm activity increased in coverage and intensity as the storms progressed southward.  The strongest storm was the one that became tied to the southward moving outflow boundary.  The largest hail size reported was teacup on I-70 near Flagler.  The strongest thunderstorm wind gust reported was 67 MPH near Kit Carson from this same storm.

Traffic across County Road 203 was temporarily restricted to one lane before the debris was cleared. Radar estimates three quarters of an inch to over an inch of rain fell with thunderstorms.

Crews reported standing water depth of 2 to 3 inches in some areas. The El Jebel RAWS site reported 0.34 inches of rain fell in approximately 30 minutes between 3:45 and 4:18 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The progression and eventual merging of two weather disturbances across western Colorado combined with a strong polar and sub-tropical jet to produce significant to heavy snowfall across most Colorado mountains and some higher valley locations. Snowfall began over the higher terrain on the evening of Wednesday, February 14th before increasing in coverage and continuing through the night of Thursday, February 15th. The heaviest snowfall fell Thursday morning and early Thursday afternoon. The support of the two upper level jets overhead enhanced snowfall rates and allowed gusty winds to mix down to the surface which resulted in periods of reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the top of the Grand Mesa ranged from 10 to 14 inches while lighter accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were reported at lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although some weather systems produced significant precipitation across the area during the month of March, these systems typically favored the higher terrain. As a result, precipitation remained below normal for many areas, especially in the southwest Colorado valleys which finished the month about an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions that had been in place since winter worsened in mid March and the drought classification was changed to extreme (D3) drought conditions across the southern portion of the Grand Mesa. The severe drought conditions persisted across the remainder of the area through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) drought conditions in mid April for the majority of the forecast zone. This situation persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm produced hail, ranging in size from nickel to half dollar size.  Heavy rain combined with a torrent of hail flooded streets and stalled vehicles in Fort Collins.  The hail accumulated to more than 6 inches in spots, clogged storm drains and caused street flooding.  The floodwaters were reportedly in excess of a foot deep in some places.  Police blocked several intersections in the city as a safety precaution.

A particularly dangerous thunderstorm moved south across Eastern Colorado during the evening near a stationary front.  As the thunderstorm moved through Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties it produced a swath of damage from the combined effects of baseball size hail and thunderstorm wind gusts.  The highest wind gust reported was 83 MPH in the northwest part of Kit Carson County.  Aside from the structural damage, many animals in the path of this storm were killed, including nine antelope south of Seibert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of buildings in Seibert sustained damage.  The post office roof had roughly half the shingles blown off.  One machine shed was completely destroyed, with the foundation blown clean.  A second machine shed, that was built using concrete blocks, only had a third of the roof blown off.  A building in town had the entire roof blown off.  A pine tree was uprooted and resting on the house next to it.  A small machine shed was flipped over and blown onto a road in town.  There were also many homes with vinyl siding destroyed on the windward side of the home from the hail and wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the latter half of the afternoon, and into the evening, strong to severe thunderstorms developed and moved north across East Central Colorado.  The largest hail reported was golf ball size near Bethune and Hale.  Meanwhile high winds moved through Burlington, which originated from storm activity in Southwest Kansas earlier in the afternoon.  A breif, weak tornado was reported north-northeast of Burlington.  No damage was reported with the tornado.

Severe thunderstorms formed the afternoon of Memorial Day.  Fourteen tornadoes were observed by spotters and chasers in the afternoon.  A rare sighting of a tornado was reported from Jackson County.  Some of the strongest storms formed across northeast Colorado, along a surface convergence boundary. The boundary extended from Kit Carson County north into Washington County, and then through Morgan and Weld counties. Intense convection that developed right on top of the boundary generated several tornadoes, with a few occurring simultaneously, especially across extreme southeast Washington County. Most of the tornadoes touched down in open fields and produced just minor crop damage. The heaviest damage occurred in Cope, in southeast Washington County, from an EF-1 tornado.  It caused extensive damage to a carport and garage on the southeast side of town, and downed some trees. The storms also produced hail up to the size of tennis balls in Washington County.  Further west, across the foothills west of Denver and across metro Denver,  instability was weaker and marginally severe hail occurred, including accumulating hail across the south metro Denver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the second of three tornadoes  that touched down simultaneously in close proximity to one another.  No damage was reported and the storm eventually crossed into Kit Carson County.

Toward the latter half of the afternoon multiple tornadoes occurred from developing storms in northwestern Kit Carson County along an outflow boundary.  At one point three tornadoes were ongoing at the same time.  The only damage reported was to an irrigation pivot.  As the storms continued to strengthen they produced large hail.  The largest hail reported was ping-pong ball size near Wray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved into the northwestern fringe of Kit Carson County from Washington County.  The tornado was moving south and ended in an open field a couple minutes after moving into Kit Carson County.  The tornado crossed the county line between CR TT and CR 0 of Washington County and CR 12 and CR 0 of Kit Carson County.  No damage was reported with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Junkins Burn Scar causing a debris flow that blocked travel across Highway 384 6 miles south of Wetmore, Colorado. A Flash Flood Warning (#46) was issued ahead of this event. Emergency management reported the debris flow at 22:45 UTC on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the Junkins burn scar caused flash flooding and debris flows across Highway 384, 6 miles south of Wetmore, CO.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes 1 mile northwest of Basalt. Difficult terrain and continued hot and dry conditions led the fire to persist for several months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lake Christine Fire began in early July from human causes, allegedly from tracer rounds fired at the Basalt Gun Range about a mile northwest of Basalt. Lack of rainfall and gusty winds allowed the fire to grow to a total of 12,588 acres before it was finally contained in the beginning of October. Periodic road closures were in effect as well as closed access in the White River National Forest. Numerous neighborhoods were evacuated. The fire destroyed three homes, a barn and an outbuilding. The three homes had a combined value of $2.65 million.

CZ_NAME: WALSENBURG VICINITY / UPPER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system produced strong winds in excess of 80 mph at times over large sections of southern Colorado.  Some of the highest reported wind gusts with this event included wind gusts between 58 mph and 64 mph near Holly, Weston, Westcreek, Sargents, Florence, Truckton, Del Norte, Gardner, Monument, Penrose, Springfield, San Miguel, Wetmore, the Royal Gorge, the Fort Carson Airport and the Air Force Academy. Wind gusts between 65 and 69 mph were observed near Creed, San Isabel, Blende, Canon City, Two Buttes, Walsenburg, La Veta Pass as well as the Alamosa, Colorado Springs, Pueblo and Trinidad airports.  Wind gusts between 70 and 79 mph were noted near Colorado City, Swissvale, La Veta Pass, Wagon Wheel Gap, the Pikes Peak Summit and the La Junta and Lamar airports.  In addition, a wind gust of 85 mph was measured near Wolf Creek Pass.

Monarch Pass received 12 inches of snow and gusty winds, while Wolf Creek pass noted 17 inches of snow with a passing storm system.

A persistent upper level trough that had been situated over the desert southwest for several days finally lifted northeast out of the region and resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms across southwest Colorado. The thundertorms were slow moving and produced heavy rain in La Plata County which led to some debris flows within the 416 fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain across Tripp Creek and Dyke Canyon in the southern portion of the 416 Fire burn scar. This led to several inches of mud and water that flowed down County Road 203 and into Durango North Village near Trimble Lane. Local news media reported half an inch of rain fell in twenty minutes with the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared with the departure of a trough of low pressure which resulted in strong radiational cooling. This allowed low temperatures to plummet to below freezing across the central and upper Yampa River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread minimum temperatures of 22 to 32 degrees F were recorded by sensors across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies allowed for low temperatures to plummet to below freezing across the Upper Gunnison River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensors for the most populated locations in the zone measured minimum temperatures of 27 to 31 degrees F. The automated sensor at the Gunnison-Crested Butte Regional Airport recorded a low of 27 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system producing strong winds, gusting in excess of 80 mph at times were experienced over sections of southern Colorado during the 23rd.  These winds caused damage to fencing and gutters.  Some of the highest reported wind gusts with this event included wind gusts between 59 mph and 64 mph near Aguilar, Lamar, Weston, Blende and Colorado City.  Wind gusts between 65 and 74 mph were noted near the Air Force Academy, Black Forest, Walsenburg and Colorado Springs. In addition, a wind gust of 83 mph was measured near Wolf Creek Pass.

Toward the latter half of the afternoon multiple tornadoes occurred from developing storms in northwestern Kit Carson County along an outflow boundary.  At one point three tornadoes were ongoing at the same time.  The only damage reported was to an irrigation pivot.  As the storms continued to strengthen they produced large hail.  The largest hail reported was ping-pong ball size near Wray.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado occurred north of CR NN, roughly 3/4 of a mile east of the CR NN/CR 4 intersection in the far northwest part of the county.  The tornado was nearly stationary in the pasture and caused no damage.

CZ_NAME: DEL NORTE VICINITY / NORTHERN SAN LUIS VALLEY BELOW 8500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system produced strong winds in excess of 80 mph at times over large sections of southern Colorado.  Some of the highest reported wind gusts with this event included wind gusts between 58 mph and 64 mph near Holly, Weston, Westcreek, Sargents, Florence, Truckton, Del Norte, Gardner, Monument, Penrose, Springfield, San Miguel, Wetmore, the Royal Gorge, the Fort Carson Airport and the Air Force Academy. Wind gusts between 65 and 69 mph were observed near Creed, San Isabel, Blende, Canon City, Two Buttes, Walsenburg, La Veta Pass as well as the Alamosa, Colorado Springs, Pueblo and Trinidad airports.  Wind gusts between 70 and 79 mph were noted near Colorado City, Swissvale, La Veta Pass, Wagon Wheel Gap, the Pikes Peak Summit and the La Junta and Lamar airports.  In addition, a wind gust of 85 mph was measured near Wolf Creek Pass.

Subtropical moisture trapped underneath a ridge of high pressure produced some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in La Plata County which led to an extensive debris flow within the 416 Wildfire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water with extensive debris about a foot deep flowed through the Mitchell Lakes drainage on the eastern portion of the 416 Wildfire burn scar due to heavy rainfall. Radar estimated half an inch to an inch of rain fell from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 16 miles northwest of Meeker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Valley Fire began in mid July due to lightning ignition about 16 miles northwest of Meeker and grew to approximately 6,310 acres before it was contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 30 miles south of Rangely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Canyon Fire began at the end of July due to lightning ignition 30 miles south of Rangely and grew to a total of 5,722 acres before it was contained.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes about a mile northwest of Basalt in early July. Difficult terrain and continued hot and dry conditions led the fire to persist for several months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lake Christine fire began in early July from human causes, allegedly from tracer rounds fired at the Basalt Gun Range about mile northwest of Basalt. Lack of rainfall and gusty winds allowed the fire to grow to a total of 12,588 acres before it was finally contained in the beginning of October. Periodic road closures were in effect as well as closures in the White River National Forest. Numerous neighborhoods were evacuated. The fire destroyed three homes, a barn and an outbuilding. The three homes had a combined value of $2.65 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire about 13 miles north of Dolores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Plateau Fire began in mid July due to lightning about 13 miles north of Dolores and grew to 19,634 acres before it was contained in early October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from unknown causes about 16 miles southeast of Meeker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cabin Lake Fire began in late July from unknown causes about 16 miles southeast of Meeker and grew to 5,975 acres before it was contained in early October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another severe hail event(with hail up to the size of softballs or 4 inches in diameter) battered El Paso county and sections of northern Pueblo county during the afternoon hours of Monday, August 6th, 2018.  ||Media reports indicate that this event caused $172 million in damages to vehicles, trees, roofs, siding, windows, skylights and other parts of homes, etc.  Especially hard hit locations included but were not limited to portions of Colorado Springs, the Broadmoor area, the Cheyenne Mountain Zoo and the communities of Fountain and Widefield.   ||In addition, 8 persons were injured when this hailstorm impacted the Cheyenne Mountain Zoo.  Sadly, 4 zoo animals perished in this storm(including a Muscovy duck, a peacock, a meerkat pup and a Cape vulture).  ||Some of the larger reported hailstones with this event during the afternoon of the 6th included hail the size of ping pong balls near Security and Falcon, hail the size of golf balls near Avondale, hail near the size of tennis balls near the Pueblo Depot, hail the size of baseballs near Fort Carson, hail the size of tea cups near Fountain and hail the size of softballs near Widefield.

Another severe hail event(with hail up to the size of softballs or 4 inches in diameter) battered El Paso county and sections of northern Pueblo county during the afternoon hours of Monday, August 6th, 2018.  ||Media reports indicate that this event caused $172 million in damages to vehicles, trees, roofs, siding, windows, skylights and other parts of homes, etc.  Especially hard hit locations included but were not limited to portions of Colorado Springs, the Broadmoor area, the Cheyenne Mountain Zoo and the communities of Fountain and Widefield.   ||In addition, 8 persons were injured when this hailstorm impacted the Cheyenne Mountain Zoo.  Sadly, 4 zoo animals perished in this storm(including a Muscovy duck, a peacock, a meerkat pup and a Cape vulture).  ||Some of the larger reported hailstones with this event during the afternoon of the 6th included hail the size of ping pong balls near Security and Falcon, hail the size of golf balls near Avondale, hail near the size of tennis balls near the Pueblo Depot, hail the size of baseballs near Fort Carson, hail the size of tea cups near Fountain and hail the size of softballs near Widefield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State patrol reported that there was debris and flooding over highway 115 at mile marker 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense dust devil damaged property at a residence on the outskirts of Orchard Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense dust devil crossed a back yard and yanked a strongly anchored trampoline up an into the air which then crashed to the ground and was totally destroyed.  The rebar anchors had been embedded into the hard and dry clay ground to a depth of 18 inches.  Roof shingles were also torn off the corner of the nearby house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the eastern portion of the zone through the end of the month while exceptional (D4) drought conditions remained over the far western portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of June. As a result, drought conditions in southwest Colorado either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the end of the month across the majority of the zone and even inched slightly further east. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions remained across the far western portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Spring Burn Scar, with particularly heavy rainfall over the Indian, Oak, and Middle Creeks. A flash flood watch was issued, as well as a flash flood warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huerfano County EM indicated visual reports of flash flooding in the highlighted region. Heavy rain over the Indian, Oak, and Middle Creeks caused flash flooding over County Road 421 near Sulphur Springs, Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second round of convection over the Spring Burn Scar produced heavy rain over the Pass Creek, South Oak Creek, and Yellowstone Creek just north of Red Wing, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second round of convection over the Spring Burn Scar produced heavy rain over the Pass Creek, South Oak Creek, and Yellowstone Creek just north of Red Wing, Colorado.  Huerfano County Emergency Manager delivered delayed reports of flash flooding over the highlighted region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Huerfano County Emergency Manager provided a delayed report of flash flooding caused by heavy rainfall over the Yellowstone Creek near County Road 530.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huerfano County Emergency Manager provided a delayed flash flood report from heavy rain over County Road 530 near Yellowstone Creek in the Spring Burn Scar area. A flash flood warning was issued about an hour before the flash flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of June. As a result, drought conditions in southwest Colorado either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions expanded further north throughout the month and worsened to exceptional (D4) status towards the end of the month across the central portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm produced hail, ranging in size from nickel to half dollar size.  Heavy rain combined with a torrent of hail flooded streets and stalled vehicles in Fort Collins.  The hail accumulated to more than 6 inches in spots, clogged storm drains and caused street flooding.  The floodwaters were reportedly in excess of a foot deep in some places.  Police blocked several intersections in the city as a safety precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado spun up 4 miles north northeast of Pueblo during the afternoon of July 12th. The report of the tornado came from a NWS employee and is backed up by video evidence provided by a Pueblo West Resident whom sent the video to a local broadcast meteorologist. The tornado was short lived and did not cause any damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee initially reported the land spout tornado. Later in the evening a local broadcast meteorologist provided video evidence that was given to her from a Pueblo West resident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late morning thunderstorm produced a tornado on a mountain in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively high-elevation tornado in the Routt National Forest was observed by numerous individuals in Routt County.  The rotational winds reached the ground initially near the 8900 foot level.  Although the rotational winds were on the ground for no more than a couple of minutes, the associated condensation funnel existed for about 10 minutes.  A debris cloud which indicated wind rotation on the ground was observed by some eyewitnesses who photographed the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted southeastern Colorado during the afternoon and evening of the 29th, producing hail up to the size of large apples as well damaging thunderstorm winds which caused tree damage, windows and windshields to be broken as well as damaging and/or destroying sheds.  Some of the larger hailstone reports with this event included hail the size of quarters near Brandon, Calhan and Crestone, hail the size of Half Dollars near Fountain as well as hail the size of Ping-Pong Balls near Bristol and Sheridan Lake.  Golf Ball hail was reported near Holly, while hail the size of Limes were measured near Fowler, Granada and Ramah.  Finally, hail the size of large Apples impact locations near Chivington in Kiowa county.

CZ_NAME: TELLER COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE ABOVE 7500 FT / PIKES PEAK BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chateau Wildfire in Teller county was fully contained on July 9. See previous month for a full narrative of this episode.

Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 23rd generated hail the size of quarters near Woodland Park, Fountain, Widefield and Pueblo.  In addition, hail up to the size of golf balls were measured near Boone.

Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of July. As a result, drought conditions in western Colorado either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional (D4) drought conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously strong northwesterly flow, monsoonal moisture, and low level southeasterly flow initiated afternoon thunderstorms over the mountains. A supercell developed over Park County in the early afternoon, developing southeasterly over Teller and El Paso County. High rainfall rates and saturated soils caused flooding, flash flooding, and debris flow along the highway 24 corridor in between Woodland Park and Colorado Springs. The same supercell was responsible for flooding south of Colorado Springs, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a supercell thunderstorm stalled a vehicle at the underpass of exit 125 and I-25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke repeatedly settled into the southwest San Juan Mountains and the Animas River Basin during the overnight and early morning hours from a combination of smoke from the 416 and Burro wildfires in the San Juan Mountains. Widespread dense smoke would diminish by the mid morning hours with localized areas of dense smoke persisting throughout each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke occurred each night and through the early morning hours across much of the southwest San Juan Mountains. The NWS Incident Meteorologist assigned to the 416 Wildfire confirmed visibilities dropped below a 1/4 mile each morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries associated with decaying thunderstorms produced severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of the Grand Valley and Eagle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimated peak wind gust of 60 mph with visibility reduced to less than a quarter mile in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries associated with decaying thunderstorms produced severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of the Grand Valley and Eagle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Eagle County Regional Airport measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph from a strong thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty outflow winds estimated up to 58 mph caused a full Rubbermaid storage shed to fall over and both of the cabinet doors to be ripped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture trapped underneath a ridge of high pressure produced some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in Archuleta and Montezuma counties. Debris flows and minor road flooding occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minor debris flow occurred over Colorado Highway 151 at mile marker 28 as a result of heavy rain. Radar estimates showed half an inch to an inch of rain fell within an hour in that area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced minor flooding across Colorado Highway 184 and 27.8 road. Nearby CoCoRaHS rainfall reports ranged from 0.70 to 0.90 of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance embedded in the flow pushed through western Colorado and brought significant moisture that produced heavy rainfall to portions of San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in mud and debris flows across Colorado Highway 145 between mile markers 75 and 80. The highway was later closed between mile markers 77 and 80. Radar estimated an inch to an inch and a half of rain fell within a short period of time in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms development over the Spring Burn Scar brought heavy rain north of Highway 160 over the Fork Creek Tributaries and the Abeyta Creek Tributaries. Delayed reports from the Huerfano County Emergency Manager confirmed flash flooding. There was a flash flood warning issued about an hour before flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Huerfano County Emergency Manager gave a delayed report of flash flooding over an un-named road that is placed along the North Abeyta Creek. Daytime thunderstorms produced heavy rain north of Highway 160, in the Spring Burn Scar region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm development and precipitation over the Spring Burn Scar caused flash flooding over local roads. One of the roads that was flooded was relatively far away from the burn scar, meaning the downstream water and debris transport from the burn scar was quiet impressive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As reported by a CoCoRaHS observer, major flooding on pass creek, at mile marker 3. Another description was that there was a 15 acre meadow moving with water, logs, and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm development and precipitation over the Spring Burn Scar caused flash flooding over local roads. One of the roads that was flooded was relatively far away from the burn scar, meaning the downstream water and debris transport from the burn scar was quiet impressive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms and heavy rain developed over the Spring Burn scar. The water drained over County Hwy 69 northeast of Gardner, Colorado, closing the highway. Report via CDOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries associated with decaying thunderstorms produced severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of the Grand Valley and Eagle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph from outflow winds associated with a strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture lingered across southwest Colorado and resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved over the area and produced 1.55 inches of rain within an hour and a half. Street flooding occurred as the storm drains were not able to handle the volume of rainfall. Water backed up into an area next to the flooded roads and flowed into the ground level floors of an apartment building. Water also pooled up to about a foot deep in a large vacant field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture lingered across southwest Colorado and resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of mud, tree branches, and other debris flowed through Hermosa Creek on the eastern portion of the 416 fire burn scar as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. At its crest the creek was running about 3 feet deeper than normal. Radar estimated half an inch to an inch of rain fell within a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough pushed through the northern Rockies and brought an increase in moisture and the coverage of afternoon showers and thunderstorms to southwest Colorado. Storms produced heavy rain and debris flow in La Plata County over the 416 fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 0.47 inches of rain fell in 16 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional (D4) drought conditions persisted across the southern portion of the zone through the month.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions spread northward across all of the the northern portion of the zone early in the month and persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the Junkins Burn scar on the afternoon of July 15th with reports of flash flooding over nearby roads. There was a Flash Flood Watch, an Areal Flood Advisory, and a Flash Flood Warning issued for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Antecedent soil conditions, the effects from the Hayden Pass burn scar, and heavy rain caused flooding over County Road 40 near Coaldale, Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture trapped underneath a ridge of high pressure produced some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in Ouray County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4 to 10 feet of mud and rock covered Colorado Highway 550 about 2 miles south of Ouray as a result of heavy rainfall. A section of the highway and cribwall that supports the highway were destroyed from the rock slides and debris flows that occurred. Highway 550 was closed for almost 24 hours until the debris was removed, and then the highway was limited to one lane for many weeks thereafter as the road was repaired.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from unknown causes 13 miles north of Durango. The fire burned on the west side of Colorado Highway 550 on some private land and on the San Juan National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 416 Fire began at the beginning of June from unknown causes about 13 miles north of Durango. The fire initially burned on the west side of Colorado Highway 550 on some private land and then spread into the San Juan National Forest. It grew to a total of 54,129 acres before it was contained at the end of July. Road closures occurred in the San Juan National Forest and also on Highway 550 at times. Operations of the Durango and Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad were halted until mid July. Numerous homes were evacuated during this wildfire.  Personal property damage was minimal and mostly confined to vegetation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lake Christine Fire began from human causes, allegedly from tracer rounds fired at the Basalt Gun Range about one mile northwest of Basalt. Lack of rainfall and gusty winds allowed the fire to grow to a total of 12,588 acres before it was finally contained in the beginning of October. Periodic road closures were in effect as well as closures in the White River National Forest. Numerous neighborhoods were evacuated. The fire destroyed three homes, a barn and an outbuilding. The three homes had a combined value of $2.65 million.
